question: What are the key security best practices for Flutter mobile apps?
answer:
Mobile app security layers:

1. Data at Rest:
• flutter_secure_storage for tokens/passwords (Keychain/Keystore)
• SQLCipher for encrypted databases
• Never SharedPreferences for sensitive data

2. Data in Transit:
• HTTPS only
• SSL/Certificate pinning (prevent MITM)
• Certificate validation

3. Code Security:
• Code obfuscation (--obfuscate flag)
• Don't log sensitive data
• No hardcoded secrets (use .env or remote config)

4. Authentication:
• Short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens
• Biometric auth (local_auth package)
• Jailbreak/root detection

5. OWASP Mobile Top 10: Improper Credential Usage, Insecure Data Storage, Insecure Communication, Insufficient Input/Output Validation

question: What is SSL/certificate pinning and how do you implement it in Flutter?
answer:
Certificate pinning prevents Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks by hardcoding the expected server certificate or public key in the app. Even if an attacker installs a custom CA, the app rejects their certificate.

Two pinning approaches:
1. Certificate pinning — pin the full certificate (changes when cert renews ~1 year)
2. Public key pinning — pin just the public key (survives cert renewals, more flexible)

Implementation in Flutter:
• Override HttpClient's badCertificateCallback
• Or use http_certificate_pinning package
• Or custom Dio adapter

Warning: If you pin incorrectly or the certificate changes without updating the app, your app loses network access. Always include a backup pin.

question: How does OAuth 2.0 with PKCE work for mobile apps and why is it required?
answer:
Public clients (mobile apps, SPAs) cannot keep a client_secret confidential — the secret would be embedded in the app binary, trivially extracted. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636) replaces the client_secret with a per-flow proof, so a stolen authorization code is useless without the matching verifier.

The flow:
1. App generates a random `code_verifier` (43–128 chars, URL-safe)
2. App computes `code_challenge = base64url(SHA256(code_verifier))`
3. App opens the auth URL with `code_challenge` + `code_challenge_method=S256`
4. User signs in; the IdP redirects back to the app with `code`
5. App exchanges the code at the token endpoint, sending the original `code_verifier`
6. The IdP recomputes SHA256(verifier), compares to the stored challenge, issues tokens only if they match

If an attacker intercepts the redirect URI (registered scheme hijack) and steals the code, they cannot exchange it because they don't know the verifier.

Other requirements for mobile:
• Use the system browser (ASWebAuthenticationSession on iOS, Custom Tabs on Android) — never WebView. WebView can be inspected, drained for credentials, and triggers anti-phishing flags.
• Use claimed/universal links for the redirect URI when possible — survives OS updates and beats most scheme-hijack attacks.
• Bind tokens to the device when the IdP supports DPoP or mTLS.

question: How does code obfuscation work in Flutter, and how do you symbolicate crashes after enabling it?
answer:
Dart compiles to AOT machine code on release builds, which is harder to read than JS/Java but still leaks identifier names in the snapshot. `--obfuscate` strips those names; `--split-debug-info=<dir>` writes the symbol map to a separate file you keep server-side.

What obfuscation does:
• Replaces class, method, and field names with short opaque tokens in the binary
• Reduces the value of static analysis for an attacker reverse-engineering your APK/IPA
• Does NOT encrypt assets, does NOT prevent decompilation, does NOT secure secrets — pair with other defenses

The symbol-map file:
• Contains the mapping from obfuscated tokens back to your real symbols
• MUST be archived per release/version — it's the only way to make a future crash readable
• Treat it like source: keep it private, ideally in your CI artifact store

Symbolicating crashes:
• On a stack trace from an obfuscated build, frames look like `package:_oz/abc123.dart 15:7`
• Run `flutter symbolize -i <stacktrace.txt> -d <split-debug-info-dir>/app.android-arm64.symbols` to recover real names
• Crashlytics, Sentry, and similar uploaders accept the symbols file and symbolicate automatically — upload from CI on each release
code_example:
# 1) Build a release with obfuscation + split debug info
flutter build apk --release \
  --obfuscate \
  --split-debug-info=build/symbols/v1_2_3

flutter build ipa --release \
  --obfuscate \
  --split-debug-info=build/symbols/v1_2_3

# 2) Archive the symbols by app version, e.g.
#    s3://acme-mobile/symbols/android/1.2.3/
#    s3://acme-mobile/symbols/ios/1.2.3/

# 3) Upload to your crash reporter (Crashlytics example)
firebase crashlytics:symbols:upload \
  --app=1:123:android:abc \
  build/symbols/v1_2_3

# 4) Symbolicate a stack trace by hand if needed
flutter symbolize \
  -i ./crash.txt \
  -d build/symbols/v1_2_3/app.android-arm64.symbols

# ── CI gate (GitHub Actions example) ─────────────────
# Fail the release pipeline if symbols aren't archived
# - run: |
#     test -f build/symbols/v${VER}/app.android-arm64.symbols
#     test -f build/symbols/v${VER}/app.ios-arm64.symbols
# - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#   with:
#     name: symbols-v${VER}
#     path: build/symbols/v${VER}
#     retention-days: 365

question: How do you detect a jailbroken / rooted device, and what should you actually do about it?
answer:
Jailbreak (iOS) and root (Android) give the user — and any malware on their device — privileges that defeat sandboxing, secure storage, and pinning. Detection is heuristic, never definitive. Treat it as a signal, not a verdict.

What you can check:
• flutter_jailbreak_detection / safe_device packages bundle the common heuristics
• iOS: presence of /Applications/Cydia.app, write access to /private, suspicious schemes (cydia://), substrate-style hooks
• Android: su binary in PATH, BusyBox, magisk artifacts, dangerous system properties (ro.debuggable, ro.secure), test-keys signing
• Both: app running with a debugger attached (`Process.isRunningInTestMode` won't catch this — you need platform calls)

What to actually do — graduated response:
• Banking/health: refuse to launch sensitive features; show "this device is not supported"
• High-stakes consumer (payments, identity): degrade — disable biometrics, force re-auth more often, require hardware-attested keys
• General consumer: log a signal, lower the trust score for risk decisions on the server side, but DON'T break the app — false positives are common (custom ROMs, dev devices)
• Don't show a leaked detection message that helps an attacker bypass the check; deny silently and rely on server-side enforcement

Key principle: client-side detection is bypassable in minutes by anyone with Frida/Objection. The real defense is server-side: device attestation (Play Integrity, App Attest), short-lived tokens, anomaly detection on transactions.

question: What are App Transport Security (iOS) and Network Security Config (Android), and when do you have to touch them?
answer:
Both platforms ship a system-level network policy that runs BEFORE your code. They block plaintext HTTP, weak ciphers, and self-signed certs by default. They also let you opt in to stricter policies (HTTPS-only by domain, custom CAs, debug-only relaxations). If your traffic looks fine in dev but mysteriously breaks in production, this is the first place to look.

iOS — App Transport Security (ATS):
• Configured in Info.plist under `NSAppTransportSecurity`
• Blocks HTTP, TLS < 1.2, and weak forward-secrecy ciphers by default
• Granular exceptions per domain via `NSExceptionDomains`
• Apple actively rejects apps that turn off ATS globally without justification
• Use `NSAllowsArbitraryLoadsInWebContent` if you specifically need cleartext WebView content but want strict native networking

Android — Network Security Config:
• XML at `res/xml/network_security_config.xml`, referenced from AndroidManifest with `android:networkSecurityConfig`
• `cleartextTrafficPermitted="false"` is the default on API 28+
• Define `<domain-config>` to whitelist self-signed certs for staging, or custom CAs for enterprise environments
• `<debug-overrides>` lets you accept user-installed CAs (Charles, Proxyman) for debug builds only — never in release

When you must touch them:
• Talking to a service that doesn't have proper TLS (legacy IoT, sensor) — narrow exception per domain
• Using a corporate root CA that the OS doesn't trust
• Debugging with a man-in-the-middle proxy in development
• Allowing cleartext during gradual TLS rollout

Never globally permit cleartext or arbitrary loads in production. Reviewers and security audits will flag it.
code_example:
<!-- iOS: ios/Runner/Info.plist — narrow ATS exception per domain -->
<key>NSAppTransportSecurity</key>
<dict>
  <key>NSAllowsArbitraryLoads</key>
  <false/>
  <key>NSExceptionDomains</key>
  <dict>
    <key>legacy-sensor.example.com</key>
    <dict>
      <!-- Plaintext only for THIS host, only because we can't fix the device -->
      <key>NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads</key>
      <true/>
      <key>NSIncludesSubdomains</key>
      <false/>
    </dict>
  </dict>
</dict>

<!-- Android: android/app/src/main/res/xml/network_security_config.xml -->
<network-security-config>
  <!-- Default: HTTPS only, system-trusted CAs only -->
  <base-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="false">
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="system"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </base-config>

  <!-- Staging: pin a self-signed cert -->
  <domain-config>
    <domain includeSubdomains="true">staging.example.com</domain>
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="@raw/staging_ca"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </domain-config>

  <!-- Debug-only: accept proxy-installed CAs (Charles, Proxyman) -->
  <debug-overrides>
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="system"/>
      <certificates src="user"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </debug-overrides>
</network-security-config>

<!-- AndroidManifest.xml:
     <application
       android:networkSecurityConfig="@xml/network_security_config"
       ...> -->

question: How do you avoid leaking sensitive data through logs, screenshots, and clipboard?
answer:
Most production leaks aren't fancy attacks — they're tokens in crash logs, customer PII in analytics events, payment forms visible in the iOS app switcher, and passwords copied to a synced Universal Clipboard.

Logs:
• Never `print` or `debugPrint` tokens, refresh codes, or full request/response bodies in release. Configure a log filter that strips Authorization headers and known PII keys before reaching the sink.
• Crash reporters (Crashlytics, Sentry) capture device state — set scrubbing rules for keys like password, otp, ssn, card.
• Don't ship a debug log file to the user via share/email without redacting first.

Screenshots / app switcher / screen recording:
• Android: `WindowManager.FLAG_SECURE` blocks screenshots, screen recording, and the recents-thumbnail. Apply only on screens that need it (login, card entry, secret notes) — global FLAG_SECURE breaks accessibility tools.
• iOS: there's no equivalent of FLAG_SECURE. Standard practice is to overlay a blurred image when the app resigns active so the recents preview doesn't expose the screen.

Clipboard:
• iOS Universal Clipboard syncs across the user's devices — secrets pasted on phone end up on Mac. Avoid putting tokens or OTPs there. If you must copy a secret, mark it expiring with `localOnly: true` (UIPasteboard.LocalOnly) and clear after a short timeout.
• Android: pre-13 the clipboard is readable by any foreground app. Same advice — don't copy secrets unless required, clear quickly.

Keyboard:
• `TextField` with `autocorrect: false, enableSuggestions: false` and `obscureText: true` for password-like fields prevents iOS/Android from caching content in their dictionaries.

question: How do you implement a robust access-token + refresh-token rotation strategy?
answer:
Long-lived bearer tokens are the most common credential-leak vector in mobile apps. The standard defense is short-lived access tokens (minutes) plus a refresh token that mints new ones — but the refresh path itself has subtle pitfalls.

Design principles:
• Access token in memory (or short-TTL secure storage). Refresh token in flutter_secure_storage. Never put either in SharedPreferences or in the URL.
• Access token short — 5–15 minutes. Refresh token longer (days/weeks) but EVERY refresh issues a NEW refresh token (rotation). The IdP invalidates the old one. If an attacker steals an old refresh token and uses it, the legitimate device gets a 401 on its next refresh — that's your detection signal.
• Single-flight refresh: under concurrent requests, multiple 401s shouldn't all kick off concurrent refreshes. Funnel them through one in-flight Future.
• On server-side revocation (logout, password change, suspicious activity): the IdP rejects the refresh token; the client must wipe credentials and force re-auth.
• Bind the refresh token to the device when possible — DPoP, mTLS, or device-attested certificates make a stolen token unusable on another device.

Flow:
1. Request returns 401
2. Acquire the refresh lock (single-flight) → call /refresh with the refresh token → receive new pair
3. Update storage atomically; release the lock; replay the original request
4. If /refresh returns 401: wipe storage, navigate to login

question: What are the security pitfalls of using WebView in a Flutter app?
answer:
WebViews host arbitrary web content with the trust of your app process. Everything you've protected with secure storage, certificate pinning, and platform sandboxing can be bypassed if you mis-configure a WebView. Several apps have shipped account-takeover bugs through WebViews.

Things to nail down:

1) Don't load untrusted URLs at all. If the source isn't a domain you fully control, prefer the system browser (url_launcher with `mode: LaunchMode.externalApplication`).

2) Disable JavaScript bridges unless required. `JavaScriptChannel`s let web code call your Dart code. Treat any channel as a public API: validate inputs ruthlessly, allow only specific origins, and never expose anything that opens secure storage, the file system, or device APIs by name.

3) Restrict navigation. Use `NavigationDelegate` (or onNavigationRequest) to block redirects to non-whitelisted hosts. Phishing pages love a forgotten redirect.

4) Cookies and storage: WebViews share cookies with the app's WebKit/WebView session. Don't reuse the same web view for an in-app webview and an OAuth page — clear cookies between flows or use a session-only configuration.

5) Mixed content + cleartext: ensure your WebView can't load HTTP subresources from an HTTPS page. Android's WebView allows mixed content by default in some versions; set `setMixedContentMode(MIXED_CONTENT_NEVER_ALLOW)`.

6) File access: `setAllowFileAccess(false)` and `setAllowFileAccessFromFileURLs(false)` unless you specifically need them. file:// URLs in a WebView can read app sandbox files.

7) Don't put auth in WebView. OAuth/OIDC flows must use the system browser (ASWebAuthenticationSession / Custom Tabs), not an embedded WebView. Apple actively rejects apps that put their sign-in into a WebView.

8) Keep the WebView engine up to date. On Android, system WebView is updated by Google Play; you still need to test against the version range your install base runs.
